course_description:
Study of cases in intellectual property (IP) in biotechnology. Criteria for patentability. Breadth of claims, reduction to practice, fair basing. Process and product patents. Terms of patent in biotechnology. Differences in patent rules between countries. Plant breeders rights. Prohibitions/exclusions from patentability in biotechnology. Compulsory licensing. Illustrative Case studies in biotechnology IP: The GTG patents, the PCR patents, Taxol patents, Neem patents, Turmeric patents, rice patents, Affymetrix patents, "Southern" patents, EST patents, sequence patents, stem cell patents. Strategies for managing biotechnology IP Analysis of the parts of a patent and a critical examination of a patent in the context of prior art. Preparation of a case study in IP conflict, dispute or debate in biotechnology.

Due to a limited number of places available in this course, there will be a selection process for enrolment with places being allocated based on the results from the prerequisite course and merit using the student's overall GPA as the criterion.

This is an advanced level course delivered by round table consultation with the lecturer, plus intra-group discussion, on an as-needed basis, to be facilitated by the course coordinator.

Students are expected to conduct independent searches for the relevant patents and case study literature.

Topics that will be covered: Criteria for patentability. Process and product patents. Terms of patent in biotechnology. Differences in patent rules between countries.

Topics that may be covered in depth depending on the particular case studies: Plant breeders rights; Prohibitions/exclusions from patentability in biotechnology; Compulsory licensing; Illustrative case studies in biotechnology IP (these will vary from year to year) and may include: The CRISPR patents, GTG patents, the PCR patents, Taxol patents, Neem patents, Turmeric patents, Golden rice patents, Affymetrix patents, "Southern" patents, EST patents, sequence patents, stem cell patents. Cases illustrating strategies for managing biotechnology IP will include analysis of the parts of a patent and a critical examination of a patent in the context of prior art, as well as anﾠanalysis of a case study in IP conflict, dispute in biotechnology.

Additional timetable information
There is no formal timetable for this course. In the first week of semester a fortnightly group consultation will be organised by the course coordinator in consultation with the students.
course_aims: This course aims to build on students'ﾠ technicalﾠ IP knowledge acquired in BIOT7033, and in other biotechnology courses byﾠ application of the knowledge to 1) examination of trade secret litigation 2) analysing an IP dispute 3) enabling the analysis of biotechnology patents and 4) devising and justifying an IP management strategy.

learning_outcomes.description: Describe several strategies for protection and commercialization of biotechnology IP
List criteria that are used to make decisions about biotechnology IP protection and management strategies and apply those criteria to make decisions
Deconstruct and analyse complex disputes in the field of biotechnology IP
List the important parts of a biotechnology patent
Demonstrate an awareness of the different interpretations of prior art in different countries in relation to biotechnology IP
Examine and evaluate trade secret litigation in biotechnology

assessment_summary.title: Examination of a trade secret litigation case
Deconstruction and analysis of a dispute
Myriad decisions in the U.S. and Australia

assessment_details.title: Examination of a trade secret litigation case
assessment_details.task_description: You will be provided with the headlines of a case involving theft of trade secrets. Prepare an essay style answer 1200 words (plus minus 10%) covering the following points. Identify the relevant court documents and case summary. Summarize the facts of the case. How was the breach identified? Identify the law(s) which have been alleged to have been breached. Summarize the courts ruling and the penalty imposed. Comment on the relevance of the penalty and anything the company could have done to prevent or mitigate the damage. Artificial Intelligence (AI) in assessment This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

Submission: Submit your assignment through the course Turnitin submission point in Blackboard.
Before submitting your assessment item to Turnitin please name your file in the following way.
FAMILY NAME_GivenName_StudentID_CourseCode_AssignmentName
For example: Bob Smith (ID: 54329876) would name his ABCD1234 essay 1 in the following way.
SMITH_Bob_54329876_ABCD1234_Essay1
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension applications must be received no later than 24 hours after the published assessment due date and time.
Read the information contained in the following links carefully before submitting an application for extension to assessment due date.
For guidance on applying for an extension, information is available here .
For the policy relating to extensions, information is available here (Part D).
Information on medical certificates
Please note the University's requirements for medical certificates here .
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date. For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period). 10% will be deducted per day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point your submission will receive a mark of zero (0) unless an extension has been approved.
In most instances one or more hurdles will apply to your assessment item so you will need to submit it to fulfil the requirements of the course regardless of how late it is and the mark you are likely to be awarded.

assessment_details.title: Deconstruction and analysis of a dispute
assessment_details.task_description: 1) Consider the Neemix patents held by W.R.Grace. 2) Download the relevant US and European patents (see blackboard website for more information). There are several patents in the family. 3) Consider the examiners report. Note briefly whether any prior art was cited against the patents. 4) List the prior art that was cited. 5) Briefly discuss the legal proceedings in the European Patent courts. 6) Briefly discuss the reasons for the finding by the European patent courts. 7) Briefly discuss the implications of this case and the lessons to be taken in relation to the interpretation of patent examiners’ search reports. 8) Discuss the different definitions of prior art in the US, Europe and Australia in relation to this case. 9) Briefly discuss the implications for IP management strategies. Maximum 5 A4 pages, 1.5 spacing 12 pt font excluding references NOTE: AFTER discussion with the course coordinator, another suitable dispute, chosen by the student in consultation with the course coordinator, may be used as the subject of this assessment item. assessment_details.title: Myriad decisions in the U.S. and Australia
assessment_details.task_description: Students will write a maximum of 5 A4 pages, excluding references (1.5 spaced) analysing and explaining the differences between the decisions of the US Supreme Court of the United States in Association for Molecular Pathology versus Myriad Genetics, Inc. (2013) , and the High Court of Australia in D'Arcy v Myriad Genetics Inc.(2015) . Please refer to the marking criterion sheet for detailed guidelines. assessment_details.title: Biotechnology IP protection strategy
assessment_details.task_description: The correct choice of IP protection strategy(s) depends on multiple factors. Patenting is only one strategy and sometimes is not the best strategy for protecting and facilitating the subsequent management of biotechnology IP. Even when patenting is used, it may be used in conjunction with other IP protection strategies. You should have read about the variety of strategies available (in the case study on commercialisation of Bt cotton, for example) or heard about them in lectures. You will also have encountered the criteria that should be applied when deciding on IP protection strategies. Your task: Two biotechnology inventions/technologies will be distributed randomly to each student. You will: a) carry out in-depth research on each of the inventions/technologies in order to understand factors such as its novelty, complexity, reverse engineerability, intended user, and the part of the technology in an overall business strategy. You will need to consult patent databases and scientific literature and perhaps the literature describing strategies already in use by companies commercialising similar technologies. You must b) design and describe an IP protection strategy for each of the inventions/technologies and c) write a detailed justification of the choice of method(s) of IP protection that are part of the strategy. In general there will be more than one justification and more than one strategy for each technology. You may even decide to place a technology in the public domain in some special circumstances (which you must describe). References must be given for all sources of information. You will then be required to present a 'client briefing' to discuss your conclusions and questions the client (examiners) may have. Report length guideline: Maximum 8 A4 pages, 12 pt font, 1.5 spaced, per invention excluding references section. Presentation length guideline: Maximum 5 min presentation with 10 min questions. Artificial Intelligence (AI) in assessment This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Regular consultation with lecturer Regular group consultations with course lecturer and other members of class, to discuss progress with assignment tasks. These consultation sessions, will be arranged in week one of the course. Topics covered will include: Advanced trade secrets; Origin of IP; Case Studies; IP Case Law; Obviousness; Strategies for IP Management Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Search Background reading Locate, download and read the relevant patents. Email lecturer to discuss downloaded documents. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
